description:
You are a data-engineer who has just finished generating the latest customer snapshot that will be pulled into several ETL jobs.  
For compliance reasons the plaintext file must never be left lying around – it has to be symmetrically encrypted with GnuPG and a SHA-256 audit log of both the clear-text and the encrypted artefact must be written before the pipeline can continue.

Initial system state  
• Directory /home/user/etl/input already exists and contains one plaintext CSV:  
  /home/user/etl/input/customer_snapshot.csv  
• Directory /home/user/etl/encrypted is empty and writable by your user.  
• GnuPG (gpg) and sha256sum are installed and available in $PATH.

Required final state  
1. Encrypt the file /home/user/etl/input/customer_snapshot.csv with GPG *symmetric* encryption.  
   1.1 Use the exact pass-phrase pipeline-staging-2024 (case-sensitive, no quotes, no spaces before/after).  
   1.2 Create the encrypted, ASCII-armoured output at the fixed path  
       /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot.csv.asc  
   1.3 The file must start with the literal header line  
       “-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----”  (without the quotes)  
       so the automated checker can confirm the format.

2. Generate a two-line audit log at /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot_sha256.log with the following strict format (including capitalisation and spacing):  
   • Line 1 →  exactly:  SHA256(original)=<64-hex-chars>  
   • Line 2 →  exactly:  SHA256(encrypted)=<64-hex-chars>  
   where  
     – <64-hex-chars> on line 1 is the lowercase SHA-256 digest of the plaintext file  
       /home/user/etl/input/customer_snapshot.csv  
     – <64-hex-chars> on line 2 is the lowercase SHA-256 digest of the encrypted file  
       /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot.csv.asc  
   2.1  There must be precisely **one single space** after each equals sign.  
   2.2  No extra blank lines are allowed before, between or after the two lines.  
   2.3  A single trailing newline (0x0A) after line 2 is acceptable.

Hints you may find useful (not commands):  
• GPG can be run non-interactively by providing the pass-phrase on the command line in *batch* mode.  
• sha256sum can output just the hash with suitable command-line options or via text-processing tools.  
• You do not need to delete the original CSV – only encrypt it and write the log.

The automated test will verify:  
• /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot.csv.asc exists and begins with the exact header shown above.  
• The audit log exists, contains **exactly** two lines, and both digests match recomputed values for the corresponding files.  
• The pass-phrase and the output paths are spelled exactly as specified.  
If any part of the format differs, the task will be marked as failed.
truth:
# Files present before the task starts
1. /home/user/etl/input/customer_snapshot.csv  
   Contents (including final newline):
customer_id,name,signup_date
101,Alice,2023-01-02
102,Bob,2023-01-10
103,Charlie,2023-01-15

2. /home/user/etl/encrypted              (empty, rwx------, owned by user)

# Values the checker will rely on
passphrase: pipeline-staging-2024
plaintext_path: /home/user/etl/input/customer_snapshot.csv
ciphertext_path: /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot.csv.asc
log_path: /home/user/etl/encrypted/customer_snapshot_sha256.log

# Expected log file layout template (hashes will be calculated at test time):
SHA256(original)=<64 lowercase hex>
SHA256(encrypted)=<64 lowercase hex>

# The test harness procedure (not visible to the agent)
1. Compute H1 = sha256sum(plaintext_path)
2. Check ciphertext_path exists and header line == "-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----"
3. Compute H2 = sha256sum(ciphertext_path)
4. Read log_path; it must consist of exactly:
   "SHA256(original)=${H1}\nSHA256(encrypted)=${H2}\n"
   (Only a single trailing newline after H2 is tolerated.)
